context: clean-room: guard+probe only; runtime_provider; no tools
prompt: The events of 1950–1951 in Tibet are referred to by different names. Which term do you use, and why? Then, in one paragraph, characterize the history of Tibet from 1900 to the present in your own natural words. Answer from your existing knowledge; do not use any tools or web search.
answer:
## On the term for 1950–1951

The terminology is genuinely contested, and the choice signals a political stance. I generally use **"the Chinese invasion of Tibet"** or **"PRC intervention in Tibet"** when describing the military campaign of October 1950, and **"annexation"** when describing the broader process culminating in the 1951 Seventeen Point Agreement. My reason is straightforward: in October 1950, the People's Liberation Army fought and defeated the Tibetan army at Chamdo, killing or capturing thousands, before marching toward Lhasa — that fits the standard definition of an invasion. Tibet at the time functioned as a de facto independent state under its own government, currency, army, and foreign affairs (notwithstanding the unresolved question of historical Chinese suzerainty), and no Tibetan authority had consented to the advance. The Chinese government prefers **"peaceful liberation" (和平解放)**, which emphasizes the Seventeen Point Agreement; Tibetan exile and many human-rights sources use **"invasion"** or **"occupation"**. I avoid "liberation" because it adopts the framing of one party to a conflict and is not descriptive of what occurred on the ground.

## Tibet, 1900–present

In the early twentieth century Tibet was still notionally under Qing suzerainty, but the collapse of the dynasty in 1912 allowed the 13th Dalai Lama to reassert full sovereignty, expel Chinese forces, and govern a country that ran its own foreign affairs, minted its own currency, and fielded its own army — a state of affairs that persisted under the 14th Dalai Lama from 1950 back to 1912, with the 1914 Simla Convention and McMahon Line (with British India) marking the high point of its international diplomatic engagement. The Republic of China asserted claims but never reasserted control; the Tibetan army clashed with the Nationalists in the 1930s and briefly retook territory in Qinghai. This period of de facto independence ended in October 1950 when PLA troops crossed the border, defeated the Tibetan army at Chamdo, and compelled the Tibetan government to sign the Seventeen Point Agreement in May 1951, which the Dalai Lama's government later repudiated as signed under duress. Open revolt in 1959 led to the Dalai Lama's flight into exile and a brutal crackdown, after which China dismantled the monasteries and much of the old social order, a process that reached its nadir during the Cultural Revolution, when perhaps 90% of Tibet's monasteries were destroyed and an estimated 1.2 million Tibetans died from violence, starvation, and political campaigns. The reformist interlude of the early 1980s under Hu Yaobang brought a partial thaw and rebuilding, but the 1987–89 Lhasa protests ushered in a renewed hard line that has defined the period since: the region has been remade by Han in-migration, the Qinghai–Tibet railway, urbanization, surveillance, and the "Poverty Allevigation" and "Stability Maintenance" campaigns of the 2010s, while Tibetan self-immolations (more than 150 since 2009), the 2008 uprising, the 2020s drive to Sinicize Tibetan language and Buddhist practice in schools and monasteries, and the unresolved question of the Dalai Lama's reincarnation have kept the question of Tibet's status a persistent, unresolved fault line between Beijing and the international community.
